sentences.text: Since Marion is the star, we should have had him resting and had Jason here.
Right.
Next time, we'll do it that way.
Isn't that usually how it's done, Jason?
[INAUDIBLE].
My name is Marion Speicher.
S-P-E-I-C-H-E-R.
My first name is M-A-R-I-O-N.
When I was born, I was named Marian, which is spelled M-A-R-I-A-N, which is a very common name in Poland for a man.
No, I just had my Hebrew name, which was Moshe.
That's the only names that we had.
We--
Yes.
I was called by-- they used to call me at home, and all my friends, [INAUDIBLE].
Where that came from, I don't know.
But I'm not using that anymore.
Nobody calls me by that name.
Because there's another friend that lives here.
He-- they call him [INAUDIBLE].
So I use Marion.
At one time, in the camps, I have changed my name.
My name was then was Marian Szpakowski, which is a pure Polish name.
Would you like me to spell it for you?
It's S-Z-P-A-K-O-W-S-K-I.
I was born on September 7th, 1925.
I am 69 plus.
I'll be 70 years this coming September.
I was born in the city called Boryslav in Poland.
Well, our hometown was small and large in a way.
It was only a population or 50,000 people, but we were a very large, industrial city because we had oil in our town.
Therefore, the town was quite well off, and it was very industrialized and modern.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The population was about one-third Poles, one-third Ukrainians, and one-third Jews.
In my family was my father, my mother, my older sister, and me.
It was just the four of us.
My father's name was Hermann.
My mother's name was Tonia.
T-O-N-I-A.
My sister's name was Lena, L-E-N-A.
And my name you know already.
My father was a cabinet maker, but he had a-- a quite nice-sized shop of-- producing furniture, where was at least 10 to 12 people working all the time.
And there were-- everything was hand made, because there wasn't too many machines at that time.
And we were quite well off.
We were not very rich, but we were not very poor.
We had quite a good life, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And things were going smooth till-- till the war started.
OK.
I went to public school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my part of town where we lived.
And-- and for six grades.
From then on, I went to gymnasium, which is equivalent to high school here.
I went there for three years, and that was the balance of my education.
Well we-- we really had no choice of subjects.
We had the-- that's not the system that was here.
Everybody had to take everything what the curriculum demanded.
And I liked math and geography, were my two main subjects that I liked.
My goals as a schoolboy, to tell you the truth, we didn't think about these things.
The situation wasn't as materialistic as in certain different societies.
So a boy of 13 or 15 years old didn't think of his future doings.
Of course, in Poland at that time, when I was started growing up 1937, 8, and 9, the situation for the Jews were becoming very difficult.
For a Jew to get accepted to a university was almost impossible.
That was a-- a very difficult thing.
And anybody that was accepted to universities, their life was one life of misery because of antisemitism.
Yeah, antisemitism we experienced, and I personally experienced from a small child on.
Starting with public school, there were always a few kids calling you, you Jew, you Jew this and you Jew that.
And-- and don't worry, the Hitler's coming.
He's going to get you.
And they used to throw stones at you.
And the way we were brought up, which was a terrible thing, never to hit back.
That was the psychology of, I think, of most of the East European Jews.
In order not to create worse antisemitism than it is.
If you should strike a Polish or Ukrainian boy, they were afraid of riots or pogroms or something like that.
So when you were hit by somebody, you just took your beating, and you ran home, and that was it.
It was a terrible situation, but that's the way we had to live.
And there was no escaping any place.
Or-- or there was no choice of-- of immigrating.
Well, of course, we didn't think of immigrating, because you were settled.
You had your family there.
You had aunts and uncles and cousins.
And the families used to be usually large.
Like my father had five brothers and five sisters.
Already-- the new generation, already, there were two children already, like my sister and myself.
But there were large families.
And there was no place to go.
So we just stayed and suffered.
And the past few years, it was getting very bad in Poland.
I'd say three or four years before the war.
Did you have any relatives or friends that you heard from who lived outside of Poland?
We, unfortunately, had no relatives living anywhere in foreign countries.
Some people-- a lot of people had relations in different countries, like mostly in America.
We had no one.
Well the interaction-- there was very little social [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] gatherings or social entertainment with non-Jews.
And mostly, Jews were separated and lived together.
And of course, when it came to business, that was a different story.
But socially, there was no connection between non-Jews and Jews.
Not in our home, anyway.
In a business way, there was connections.
As [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were, as it goes into business.
Anybody needed any furniture or whatever, we used to-- my father used to do work for the city.
And so things were going not bad til about '37, '38.
After that, things were getting very bad.
No, I wouldn't say my family was religious.
We observed a kosher home.
And we were really traditional.
My father didn't go to shul except for high holidays.
I used to go to shul with my father, my mother at high holidays.
We used to-- it was a big-- holidays was a big thing by us, especially Passover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was beautiful.
We used to bring down the dishes and the pots and pans from the attic.
And everybody got new clothes for Passover.
That was traditional, I guess.
Got new suit, new shoes.
And the weather didn't ever-- was favorable in April.
Used to be always rains.
But it was a very nice holiday, because the families used to get together for the Seder and so on.
So it was quite pleasant.
And of course we had a lot of families living in the neighborhoods.
Within a five-minute walk or ten-minute walk, you can always find an uncle or a cousin or somebody.
So things were-- the youth-- the life up to 1939 was quite pleasant.
Well, we had-- did have an inkling of what was happening because, in 1938, Hitler chased out all the Polish Jews that did not become citizens in Germany.
And these people slowly came back to their families, and they were telling us stories of what's happening in Germany.
But of course, till 1939, things were bad for the Jews, but there weren't-- there were no actual killings or burning and crematorium or concentration camps.
Even some Jews, when they got in to the first camp-- I think was Dachau-- and some of them were arrested and then released later.
So it wasn't as bad as it started in 1939.
Well, she was two years older.
She was born on January 1st, 1923.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she went also to public school.
And of course, I should mention, that we had-- our public schools were separate for boys and for girls, even though they were adjoining buildings.
And after that, she went to gymnasium.
And she was a little older, so she was going out already with boys.
But we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not quite-- our youth was not quite as advanced as youth-- young people here today, because a girl of 12, 13 years old was still a child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, when the war broke out, my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had very difficult times.
My mother was a housekeeper, like most of the women.
I don't know of anybody that worked.
And she kept the house.
She had to cook and bake for Seder or Shabbos.
She used to bake cakes and all kinds of things.
And-- and every day, my mother used to go to the market to buy fresh-- everything fresh, because there was no refrigeration.
So there was-- you couldn't keep meat or butter or other things for very long.
Maybe for a day or two.
Of course, in the winter, it was no problem, because everything froze.
But she used to go every day to the market, buy your stuff, prepare a dinner.
Our dinner was at 12 or 1:00.
I don't remember anymore.
I think 1:00, when we came from school.
And-- and in the evening was always a light meal.
So the main meal of the day was the dinner around 1:00.
And everybody, all four of us, had to sit down around the table.
There were no excuses.
Everybody had to be there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then my mother used to-- I don't know.
Sometimes she read a little bit.
Sometimes she used to sit with the neighbors and talk.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents used to go, like, Saturday or Sunday, I don't remember, to the movies.
We had a few movie houses in our town.
There were, in fact, three of them.
And they used to go to the movies.
I used to go to movies too, and my sister also, separately.
Everybody.
Sometimes I went with my parents, and sometimes I just got a few cents and I went for a double feature, which take-- took probably four hours.
And that was the very quiet life in a small city.
Yes.
In the past, as I was getting a little bit older, there was quite a big Zionist movement in our town.
And they formed in quite a few organizations.
Each one had a different idea or ideal.
Some were pro-Israel.
Some were more a left-wing organization, also pro-Israel.
And there was a HaNoar.
There was Akiva.
There was the Shomer, which was a left-wing organization.
And a few more that I don't remember their names.
As a matter of fact, I also joined one.
I joined the Akiva.
And that was a social club, like.
And we had a few older guys teaching us about Israel.
And there was a big movement to immigrate to Israel.
And we-- I was what, maybe 12, 13 years old?
We used to march.
And being taught Hebrew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we played ping-pong in the place, and we had fun.
It was more or less like a social club.
We started getting very aware in '37, '38, and '39.
And we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the head of Polish government, the President was just a figurehead.
And we had a Marshal, Pilsudski, which, when he was governing since 1918 or 1920-- I don't know exactly-- the Jews were treated quite fairly.
It's P-I-L-S-U-D-K-I.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he died, I think, in 1936.
At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Marshal's job was taken over by [INAUDIBLE].
I won't even try to spell it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the parliament started passing laws which were detrimental to the Jews.
And the worst law that they passed was the forbidding slaughter-- kosher slaughtering of cattle, because they claimed that it was-- was inhuman, the ritual slaughter.
And after a big to-do, they allowed to slaughter only once a week so the Jews would have some meat for Seder or Shabbos.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise, antisemitism was growing by laps and leaps, and we felt it in schools.
And every official [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] entity, whether it was the tax office or post office or the mail, everybody started openly taking advantage of the Jews.
And things were really getting bad.
The newspapers and the radio and the-- and the parliament was really knowing places.
And they just couldn't wait for the Hitler to come in and finish off the Jews.
And that's all you heard is, they were saying it in Polish which was rhyming-- run away, Jew, because Hitler is coming.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was-- that lasted til 1939.
No.
Our immediate neighbors behaved very well, because we lived with them together.
And there was really no problem with the immediate neighbors.
That was just in general going on, and felt everywhere.
In '39, the war broke out on September 1st.
And a few days later, maybe 10 days later, you know, the Polish army couldn't stand up against the Germans.
So they were just running away and running.
And most of them were running away to Romania.
And about-- I don't know exact dates, but somewhere around 10 days after the war broke out, the Germans walked into our town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and right away, they started taking-- they took a few prominent Jews.
They put them against the wall-- about 10 of them, I think.
They put them against the wall.
They did not shoot them then.
They demanded, I think, money or whatever it was.
They released them.
And there was panic all over town.
And that lasted for about seven, eight days.
At that time, Hitler made the pact with Russia and they divided Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a few days of the Germans in 1939, the Russian army came in and the Germans withdrew to their part of Poland.
And we were on the eastern part of Poland, which is actually now Ukraine.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were under Russian occupation or whatever you want to call it.
We were liberated by the Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Russians came in, we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually things improved greatly.
Because no one was afraid of his life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jews stayed in their homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things economically were not as good as they should be.
But if you had enough food-- was nothing the best food-- but you had enough bread, you had enough potatoes.
You were not hungry.
And they took away all the big stores, the factories, the shops.
They-- they requisitioned everything.
But a few people, very rich ones, or political, were sent to Siberia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the majority of people were left alone.
Whoever had a business, like my father, they took away his business, and he had to go to work with his hands.
Which wasn't a tragedy in a way.
But nobody threatened your life.
And-- and there was no fear of being killed or beaten or shot.
If you didn't mix into any politics, they left you alone.
And there was no problem of anything.
We continued school.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And life was going on at a pace that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't very, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very quick, or-- or-- or we just lived from day to day.
